prompt: What was 3M's total net sales for the three months ended June 30, 2024, per its Q2 2024 10-Q?
expert_answer: 3M's total net sales for the three months ended June 30, 2024 were $6,255 million.
supporting_facts: Per the Consolidated Statement of Income (Loss) on page 4 of 3M's Q2 2024 Form 10-Q (for the quarterly period ended June 30, 2024): net sales for the three months ended June 30, 2024 were $6,255 million, compared to $6,283 million for the three months ended June 30, 2023. Year-to-date net sales for the six months ended June 30, 2024 were $12,271 million versus $12,338 million for the comparable prior-year period. Note: results are presented on a continuing-operations basis following 3M's April 2024 separation of Solventum, with prior-period figures reclassified accordingly.
justification: Direct extraction from the Consolidated Statement of Income (Loss). No computation is required. The figure of $6,255 million is read directly from the 'Net sales' line for the three-month period ended June 30, 2024.

prompt: As per Boeing's FY2024 10-K, by what % did Commercial Airplanes segment revenue grow YoY, and which two factors does management cite in MD&A as the largest contributors? Reconcile any apparent discrepancy between the segment table.
expert_answer: Boeing's Commercial Airplanes (BCA) segment revenue did not grow year-over-year in FY2024 — it declined by approximately 32.6%, falling from $33,901 million in 2023 to $22,861 million in 2024 (a decrease of $11,040 million). The two factors management cites in MD&A as the largest contributors to the BCA revenue decline are: (1) lower deliveries across all commercial aircraft programs (commercial deliveries fell from 528 airplanes in 2023 to 348 in 2024), driven primarily by the 53-day IAM District 751 work stoppage from September 13 to November 4, 2024 and the slower 737 production rate following the January 2024 737-9 in-flight emergency, and (2) customer considerations and accommodations related to the 737-9 grounding. There is no material discrepancy between the Summary of Business Segment Data table and the MD&A discussion: the table reports the absolute revenue decline and the MD&A attributes that decline to the same two operational drivers.
supporting_facts: Per Boeing's FY2024 Form 10-K (for the fiscal year ended December 31, 2024): Commercial Airplanes segment revenue was $22,861 million in 2024 versus $33,901 million in 2023 (Summary of Business Segment Data). Total company revenue was $66,517 million in 2024 versus $77,794 million in 2023, a decrease of $11,277 million primarily due to lower revenues at Commercial Airplanes and Defense, Space & Security, partially offset by higher revenues at Global Services. Total commercial airplane deliveries: 348 in 2024 versus 528 in 2023. The MD&A states that the decrease in Commercial Airplanes revenue was due to lower deliveries across all programs and specific customer considerations related to the 737-9 grounding. The 53-day IAM 751 work stoppage (September 13 to November 4, 2024) paused production of all commercial aircraft other than the 787 produced in Charleston. The Alaska Airlines 737-9 in-flight door plug separation occurred on January 5, 2024 and resulted in FAA-imposed production rate caps of 38 per month on the 737 program. 1 basis point = 0.01 percentage point.
justification: Step 1 — Year-over-year percentage change calculation: ((Current Year - Prior Year) / Prior Year) × 100 = ((22,861 - 33,901) / 33,901) × 100 = (-11,040 / 33,901) × 100 = -0.32566 × 100 = -32.57%. Rounded to one decimal: a 32.6% decrease (the prompt's 'grow' framing is corrected in the answer because the segment revenue declined, it did not grow). Step 2 — MD&A factor identification: read the BCA segment discussion in the Results of Operations section of MD&A. Management identifies two named drivers: (i) 'lower deliveries across all programs' and (ii) '737-9 grounding customer considerations.' These are the two factors cited as largest contributors. Step 3 — Reconciliation between segment table and MD&A: the Summary of Business Segment Data table presents the absolute dollar figures ($22,861M vs $33,901M); the MD&A narrative explains the operational drivers behind those figures (delivery shortfall, 737-9 customer considerations). Both reference the same underlying events (IAM strike impact on deliveries, 737-9 grounding consequences), so no reconciling adjustment is needed — the table and MD&A are internally consistent.

prompt: Using the most recent FY 10-Ks of NVIDIA (NVDA), Advanced Micro Devices (AMD), and Intel (INTC), compute each company's R&D intensity (R&D expense ÷ total revenue) for the most recent fiscal year and the prior fiscal year. Identify (a) which company has the highest R&D intensity in the most recent year, and (b) which company saw the largest YoY change in R&D intensity, expressed in basis points.
expert_answer: R&D intensity (R&D expense ÷ total revenue) by company: NVIDIA — FY2026 (ended Jan 25, 2026): 8.57% ($18,497M / $215,938M); FY2025 (ended Jan 26, 2025): 9.90% ($12,914M / $130,497M); YoY change: -133 bps. AMD — FY2025 (ended Dec 27, 2025): 23.36% ($8,091M / $34,639M); FY2024 (ended Dec 28, 2024): 25.04% ($6,456M / $25,785M); YoY change: -168 bps. Intel — FY2025 (ended Dec 27, 2025): 26.06% ($13,774M / $52,853M); FY2024 (ended Dec 28, 2024): 31.16% ($16,546M / $53,101M); YoY change: -510 bps. (a) Intel has the highest R&D intensity in the most recent fiscal year at 26.06%, exceeding AMD (23.36%) and NVIDIA (8.57%). (b) Intel saw the largest YoY change in R&D intensity at -510 bps in absolute magnitude, larger than AMD (-168 bps) and NVIDIA (-133 bps). All three companies showed declining R&D intensity year-over-year — for NVIDIA and AMD this reflects revenue growing faster than R&D spending, while for Intel it reflects an absolute reduction in R&D expense (down approximately 16.8%) on roughly flat revenue.
supporting_facts: NVIDIA — Per FY2026 Form 10-K (fiscal year ended January 25, 2026), Consolidated Statements of Income: Revenue $215,938 million in FY2026 and $130,497 million in FY2025; Research and development expense $18,497 million in FY2026 and $12,914 million in FY2025. AMD — Per FY2025 Form 10-K (fiscal year ended December 27, 2025), Consolidated Statements of Operations: Net revenue $34,639 million in FY2025 and $25,785 million in FY2024; Research and development expense $8,091 million in FY2025 and $6,456 million in FY2024 (MD&A: 'Research and development expenses of $8.1 billion in 2025 increased by $1.6 billion, or 25%, compared to $6.5 billion in 2024'). Intel — Per FY2025 Form 10-K (fiscal year ended December 27, 2025), Consolidated Statements of Operations: Net revenue $52,853 million in FY2025 (down $248 million from FY2024) and $53,101 million in FY2024; Research and development expense $13,774 million in FY2025 and $16,546 million in FY2024 (MD&A: 'R&D expenses were $13.8 billion in 2025, $16.5 billion in 2024 and $16.0 billion in 2023'). 1 basis point (bp) = 0.01 percentage point; 100 bps = 1 percentage point.
justification: Formula: R&D intensity = R&D expense ÷ total revenue, expressed as a percentage. Conversion: percentage points × 100 = basis points. NVIDIA FY2026: 18,497 / 215,938 = 0.085658 = 8.5658% (856.58 bps). NVIDIA FY2025: 12,914 / 130,497 = 0.098960 = 9.8960% (989.60 bps). NVIDIA YoY change: 856.58 - 989.60 = -133.02 bps, rounded to -133 bps. AMD FY2025: 8,091 / 34,639 = 0.233581 = 23.3581% (2,335.81 bps). AMD FY2024: 6,456 / 25,785 = 0.250378 = 25.0378% (2,503.78 bps). AMD YoY change: 2,335.81 - 2,503.78 = -167.97 bps, rounded to -168 bps. Intel FY2025: 13,774 / 52,853 = 0.260605 = 26.0605% (2,606.05 bps). Intel FY2024: 16,546 / 53,101 = 0.311595 = 31.1595% (3,115.95 bps). Intel YoY change: 2,606.05 - 3,115.95 = -509.90 bps, rounded to -510 bps. Comparison for (a) — most recent year R&D intensity: Intel 26.06% > AMD 23.36% > NVIDIA 8.57%, so Intel is highest. Comparison for (b) — absolute magnitude of YoY change in basis points: |Intel -510| > |AMD -168| > |NVIDIA -133|, so Intel has the largest YoY change.
